author: @peterbeer8657
text:
Your design is like some other already real wind power turbines being used and commercially produced. But as you said it, the wind first blow against the sides before it's being pushed through the fan. Why did you make the fan so small? The amount of air being caught by the sides is a lot more, so the fan is allowed to be much bigger. In fact why do you have all the compartments, why don't you just capture the wind in the half bucket, half empty bucket it becomes, and funnel the wind through the fan that way? 
As the bucket catches wind it may slow down a boat, van, camper or sorts unless if you have it just sitting on a rooftop or your vehicle is standing still. So what I'm saying is this is't that brilliant to use with a moving vehicle. Still it's smart if you just put it on top of a roof.

author: @vociferonheraldofthewintermist
text:
Right now, I don't feel so stupid. I had an idea when electric cars came out about a system of mini-wind turbines to help generate power. Even mounting a half dozen turbines on the roof to charge a battery like the Jackery to power non-car things (phone charger, cigarette lighter - yes I'm old - dome light) would help some. 

After all, the vehicle is moving. Wind is happening. Why not recapture some of that energy and use it? I know about thermodynamics, but we're dealing with drag regardless. I'm not talking about 'free' power, I'm talking about getting back some energy that we're letting go now. Put power generating turbines in places like the grill or the side mirrors where we're already dealing with drag. 

Whenever I told someone about it, they'd throw the thermodynamics thing at me and that's never made sense. It would be like someone developing a solar paint that captures the light hitting the vehicle and people saying, "Can't work. Thermodynamics." No. You're just keeping light that would've bounced off anyway.

With the lighter weight materials that we have to play with now, it might work if we're letting wind pass through a spot where it normally would've had to go around. Even if it's a break even between drag, charge, and weight , that's still power that can be used NOW, while the car's in motion. Even with the heavy batteries, electric cars already have them. We're not adding a battery for the turbines.

author: @donbozarth6627
text: Your concept is more than interesting but I see an idea that may make a small improvement in the net forced air velocity that could easily be added to your hyperbolic cones. If you were to generate a vortex through the wind stream the output of the cone would carry the vortex at some n velocity increasing the rotational speed of the end effector which would be your impeller. There would be a requirement to determine the attack angle of the of the impeller of course and determine the swept angle of the vortex generator in the cone. Imagine the internal swept surface area of your cone having a series of 3~8mm swept fins along the internal surface swept in the same direction as the input of your impeller. As the wind is ingested by the funnel the fins would begin to spin the air at a speed that would be variable by the amount of sweep from the widest to smallest opening. In my untested opinion the net of the output from the cone would increase the efficiency of the impeller and allow for a higher rpm net and higher net torque. How much is questionable but if you could gain a net 20~30% speed/torque increase you could drive a much larger generator utilizing the increased nm. Regardless, your concept is proven and leads me to much grander questions in areas of improvement in efficiency. Thanks for your candor and obvious sense of what is humorous in the mind of an engineer. Lol! Our humor is dark but also enlightening even at the expense of sarcasm. I sincerely hope I have expressed in near enough terms what my minds eye sees as how to implement the vortex upon the cones/funnels.

author: @BenEehayeh
text:
I have been thinking similarly, for cars and boats to be fitted with wind turbines. I have been thinking of a DNA type helical blade, 8-10" diameter on the roof of a vehicle across the passenger compartment, incorporating a DC generator for local use.
Designing coils for DC will provide several advantages.
1. Low magnetic friction
2. No AC to DC conversion losses
3. Direct to storage batteries
4. Lower torque to overcome friction

When power generation is within a short distance of the storage medium, DC is the better solution. As an example, windmill to a shed that contains batteries.

author: @driftlessheights6177
text:
I love your enthusiam and thought.

A few scaling questions please.

The general equation of a parabola is: y = a(x-h)^2 + k , where ('h','k') denotes the vertex and 'a' denotes the curviness of the parabola.

What values did you use for these pieces?
Was it just a guess or did you get optimal values from some engineering source?

How did you determine that optimal distance between each parabolic panel?
I figure air resistance will increase as they get closer but efficiency may decrease when they get too far apart.

Would one use different values if you scaled this up to, say a 6 foot diameter?
Would the diameter of the hole in the middle increase at the same rate?

I have been watching another channel where they build car bonnets, etc. from carbon fiber. ( Easy Composites Ltd )
It would be fun to make a mold and make as many of these parabolic panels as I want.
But the mold is a lot of work so validity of scaling is important.

Let me know what you think.

Thanks, Tom

author: @gkdresden
text:
I doubt that this design will work in a useful way. According to fluid dynamics you concentrate the moving air of a large area to a much smaller area under acceleration. But where is the energy coming from? Obviously from the internal energy of the gas flow. So the static pressure and the temperature of the gas flow must go down. So you will get a lower inlet pressure at the turbine wheel than its outlet pressure.

So the air flow concentrator will not work. You will never force more air through the turbine wheel than in the case that you put it directly in the air flow. In fact you will get a reduced air flow. You can do CFD simulations to find exactly this.

author: @giles1962
text:
Rather than having the rear shield, which would need to rotate in-line with the wind direction, could you add vanes to the cones?  These vertical vanes would stop the wind going straight through the cones and then divert the wind in either an up, or down direction.  That way the unit would be omni directional.
I think a down wind direction may be best, that way the turbine, with the blades, magnets and coils could all be located closer to the ground, or it’s attachment point.
One last thing, maybe a rotating shield could also be used, after all.  However, instead of behind the cones, I would locate the shield at the wind outlet side, and then in the opposite direction.  In this configuration the shield would block the oncoming wind and act like a venturi (creating a lower air pressure behind the turbine), this should then actually suck the wind out of that area, adding more power to the unit.

author: @fredturk6447
text:
I don’t think this is likely very useful. In order to get any power out it would need to be scaled up considerably. If you consider a wind turbine about a metre diameter which is what’s needed to get any significant power you have an area of pi x r x r or about 0.75 metres. So the projected area of this device would need to be similar, about a metre high and 0.75 metres wide. So it’s going to have significant volume given it is a cylindrical shape. 
It’s just a fact that the energy of moving air is not very high at lower wind speeds and to compensate you need a big collection area.

replies.author: @traviswansai2784
replies.text:
@NebulaM57 3D printing  can be very frustrating due to all the fine settings that need to be checked. I think You should check different  settings using a Benchy.
A few years ago I eliminated most of my print failures with extruder temperature calibration, and slower print speeds. Print speeds should not change from walls and layers. Every time the print speed changes the nozzle pressure will change requiring the extruder temperature to fluctuate.
Curing your filament with a food dryer before printing can be a problem solver. 
Hope this helps!

author: @NameIsDoc
text:
Another thing you should look at is differential pressure system based wind generation.

 there are a few new wind turbines where instead of using the wind to turn an turbine outright. they instead use hollow wingforms attached to long tubes with holes in the low pressure areas of the wingform. When air is blown over the wing it creates a small vacuum like we use for lift in airplanes. However in this case with the holes in the right place the vacuum sucks the air out of the pipe attached to the wing rather then creating lift. you can then stick a turbine at the end of the pipe to generate power.

replies.author: @BenEehayeh
replies.text:
Propeller blades are inefficient.
The area of force is much greater than the area of the blades. 
If the blades had a larger surface area they could convert more energy from the wind.

replies.author: @robertjung8929
replies.text: @BenEehayeh wind turbines can extract up to 50% of energy from the wind, following your logic the blades should cover 50% of the swept area , but they not. btw... did you know the wind turbine is not "pushed"  around by the wind force but rather "pulled" around by aerodynamic lift of the blades generated by the blowing wind ? so this concept turbine of Robert will not increase efficiency (it might actually decrease it due to friction loses while redirecting the flow), but it will transform the "dynamic" swept area with flying blades into a "static" swept area with no moving parts (hence safer). i think it's a neat idea definitely worth of investigating, but i don't think it's anything revolutionary :)

replies.author: @BenEehayeh
replies.text:
@robertjung8929 What is the cut in speed for propeller blades?
8-10 miles per hour winds.
Non-propeller designs, like the helical DNA shaped blades, begin to rotate in 3 mile per hour winds.
Helical wind turbines have been in use in Europe for 20+ years.

text: Hmmm. The power from a conventional propeller is generated at the tips which in turn produces leverage, which is why the larger the propeller diameter (think wind farm) the greater the power. The whole thing would need to be much larger, including the turbine.

replies.author: @anysailer
replies.text: I agree, a great design, and I believe it will prove to be quite robust. Conventional wind turbines generate power due to the the lever effect when a column of moving air strikes the angled blade surfaces set perpendicular to the direction of the wind.  The lever effect is translated into a very powerful twisting force (torque) at the fixed point or hub of the blade assembly, giving rise to the relatively slow (30-40 rpm) rotational movement that we see.  The component that normally generates the electrical power is an alternator which operates at a much higher rotational speed (1500 rpm).  Consequently a very large multi stage gearbox is required between the rotating blade assembly and the alternator.  This is the week point in most wind turbine designs as an immense amount of heat is generated inside these gearboxes due to the vast energy transferred in a relatively small space.  These gearboxes which are the size of small vans must increase the drive speed from the blades from 30rpm to 1500rpm, and are filled with oil for cooling and lubrication.  To prevent an over speed event in high winds, the blades can be feathered to reduce their rotational efficiency and the nacelle can be turned to reduce the effect of the wind on the blades, but as the blades remain constantly exposed to the prevailing winds, any failure of the built in control systems that prevent overspeed events, may result in excess stress, build up of heat and eventual seizing of the gearbox with disasterous results (fires, blades spinning out of control, breaking and even complete collapse of the tower).  Roberts design has non of these features, and also has the advantage over other types of horizontal axis wind turbines (Savonius and Darrieus types) that there are no moving parts exposed directly to the elements.  Also the open side can easily be closed off either partially or completely using a semicircular slide during excessively high winds, so this design should prove considerably more weather and wind proof than other types of wind turbines, in fact it would prove ideal for rough weather and high wind speed environments, and could be promoted as such. The only down side is that its power capacity is limited to the relatively small area of incidental wind compared to the vast cylindrical column of wind that a conventional wind turbines blades are exposed to as defined by the circular area swept by the blades rotating around the hub. But even this limitation can be over come by stacking units to form a 'turbine tower', and even arranging towers next to each other to create 'turbine walls'. This could be used instead of conventional cladding on skyscrapers and other tall structures.  My apologies, I hope you don't mind my very long reply, but I confess, I really love this design.

replies.author: @RubenKelevra
replies.text:
The limit is that you compress/speed up the air to move through the smaller area of the turbine. There's quite some loss attached to this. So using a device like this makes only sense if you otherwise cannot achieve such a large area (safely).

The compression of the wind will increase depending on how much energy you extract - so your electrical load - which would need to be managed to be in an optimal efficiency range for the blade pitch/optimal RPM for the generator.

If you want to simplify the system electrically, you could use centrifugal force to adjust the blade pitch to keep it in an optimal pitch for the wind pressure (aka wind speed).

So you get a variable RPM but an optimal pitch to capture the wind with optimal efficiency.

The variable RPM can then be converted to an optimal RPM of the generator with a CVT – instead of using magnets in the rotor itself (which is a stupid idea to begin with).

author: @DUKE_of_RAMBLE
text:
Looks like we're *_almost_* making something nearing that of a (Nicola) Tesla Turbine.
That's just a stack of discs with a gap in between each, though, no impeller or any other sort of blades. 

A great example of an impromptu one, is a computer Harddisk Drive.
Take the cover off, power it up, and witness the immense amount of air it moves!

Makes me wonder if a Christmas Stack of discs, mounted at am angle (so the leading edge is vertical) would work out... I think it would be self-orienting, thanks to the shaft's angle (mounted on a platform that can traverse around), and with the angled discs I think it would help to funnel air into (and between) them.

What I like about that velocity stack shape, though, is I think it *_might_* act like a compressor in a Tesla Turbine...? Although the bottom of the next "velocity disc" would need a flat bottom part way in, to look more like the inlet side of a Turbo...

Just spitballing.

replies.text: videos aren't good at capturing rotation because they're 25 still images per second. They will always give an unrepresentative view & at certain speeds will show a rotating object as motionless. Much better is some sort of gauge. A simple analogue gauge is a magnet in an aluminium cup, one part rotates with the mechanism & the other holds a pointer & acts against a spring, the eddy currents in the cup drag the magnet. It works with either part rotating.

replies.author: @Emily35-Official
replies.text:
I would argue that watt output is a sufficient measurement as at the end of the day, that is all we really care about.

Having a generator built up, with a couple wires running down to a test bench, you only need to measure the power output of as you apply a progressively larger load and see where the wattage peaks before stalling the turbine.

A large fan, such as a positive pressure ventilation fan used by fire departments, could be used within an enclosed environment as a static source of wind, a conventional wind turbine tested and your unit tested to compare.

My long term life goal is to build a self sufficient electrical powered sailboat (with a diesel generator for backup, because I'm not totally crazy, but hope I never need it).

Wind turbines are commonly under powered, noisy and bulky compared to solar and hydro power generation, especially when looking at a huge power budget of induction stove tops, convection microwave, electric incinerator toilet AND an electric drive motor.

Not to mention, fridge / freezers, washing machine clothes dryer and electric heating / cooling (reverse cycle marine AC with sea water heat exchanger most likely).

A couple of these high on the mast may be quite useful, IF the power output is worth the extra weight up the mast.

Another thing you might consider, is some way to turn it off.

On a following wind, capturing and converting to electrical power will also produce some mechanical force helping propel the boat, but any wind coming from forward of 90 degrees will also effect a force on the boat, in the wrong direction.

In most cases, this won't matter that much, but on light winds where you want everything to work for you, anything working against you is bad.

BTW, hi. Random YouTube recommendation brought me here.

replies.author: @gilray1977
replies.text:
​ @Emily35  ~
 "On a following wind, capturing and converting to electrical power will also produce some mechanical force helping propel the boat, " <~~~~~ You perhaps are not considering the difference between 'true wind' and relative wind'. Most wind generators do not produce much if any energy when sailing down wind in anything less than a full gale. This is because the 'relative wind' moving over the boat is radically reduced from what the 'true wind' is. The size and 'projected area' of the generator Robert is working with would not add any measurable, positive influence / thrust to a sail boat going down wind ! 
 "but any wind coming from forward of 90 degrees will also effect a force on the boat, in the wrong direction."
 Well Emily, if you want to have "fridge / freezers, washing machine clothes dryer and electric heating / cooling" <~~ You are obviously not talking about a 'tiny live aboard'. In order to have that level of decadence aboard your dream boat, you are talking at least a 45' boat. That would average out to a displacement of 30 - 35K lbs. At that displacement it would take a huge (like 8' tall) generator to create enough windage / drag to be considered an impediment to upwind performance. You did mention putting generator(s) up high in the rig and this would greatly improve their performance by getting them aloft into cleaner, higher velocity wind. However even putting one at say mid mast height, you would need to have one that was over 2.5' tall to even start to see any significant sailing performance loss due to windage. If you are talking about a boat with ""fridge / freezers, washing machine clothes dryer..." You are talking about a big, heavy displacement, cruising boat that has pretty low and slow performance to begin with. So the windage 'knock' on your proposed boat would be extremely negligible if even register able on a generator of less than gargantuan proportion :)

replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering I didn't know what a Dorade vent was, and on first view I thought it was an exhaust vent. On second look at how they work, I see they're a vent to bring air in a ship.
My thought was a 'reverse' dorade style vent that forms a vacuum exhaust, increasing the speed of air flowing through the turbine. A vent on top that swivels away from the wind flow, directing the generator air downwind. Instead of the opening pointing into the wind, it points away from the wind.
There would likely be an increase in generator speed and torque if the air flowing through the generator had an easy place to go.
The commercial examples at 0:24 and 0:29 do not show that. The first one has a static exhaust pointing in one direction, the second one is set up more like yours where the compressed air exhausts directly into the flow of air around the generator, in an already denser flow of fluid (air) rather than a partial vacuum.
I don't know if I'm explaining myself properly. I wish I could upload a graphic representation of what I see in my mind.

replies.author: @PBGetson
replies.text:
I had never even heard the term dorade, and after seeing them, got me thinking about how they work. I envisioned an exhaust, not an intake. See my comment to Robert.
Thanks for the flash of imagination.

replies.text:
@markseamans4682 Well, you can call it "a series". Those are building blocks and Robert explained this a few times in his clips. It's like a Lego for a bit bigger kids😁
This part is about catching wind. 
You can find a part about different ways of forcing wind to move something. 
Then there is a part about how to change that move into electricity.
When you put everything together, you will make a wind generator. But you will remember it has different parts and understanding how they work will help you to slap one together just for your specific needs.
It's similar to one bushcraft rule told by Mr. Mors Kochansky: the more you know, the less you carry.
And Robert is giving this knowledge here. With a big smile and enthusiasm😃
